title: Samsung’s New R95H Isn’t the Best New Micro RBG TV on the Block
gist: Wired rates Samsung's $3,200 Micro RGB R95H TV a 6/10, finding it easy to set up with a good remote and strong AI Soccer Mode, but outclassed on color accuracy and picture-quality customization by the competing LG Micro RGB Evo and Hisense UR9.

insights.claim: Wired rated the Samsung Micro RGB R95H 6 out of 10, concluding it is a good TV but does not offer the precise picture-quality controls and vibrancy of competitors like LG.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Establishes Samsung as currently trailing rivals in the emerging micro-RGB TV category on core performance metrics, relevant to competitive positioning in premium display hardware.

insights.claim: At $3,200 for the 65-inch model, the Samsung R95H cannot compete with the LG Micro RGB Evo, whose smallest available size is 75 inches at $4,500 (matching the R95H's 75-inch price).
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Direct price/size comparison shows Samsung offers a smaller entry size at a lower price point but loses on picture quality, a relevant product-line tradeoff for consumer electronics positioning.

insights.claim: Samsung's anti-glare technology was identified as the R95H's key weakness, making movies and some video games look too subdued and reducing how much color/contrast could be improved via picture-mode tweaks compared to the LG or Hisense models.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific engineering tradeoff (anti-glare coating vs. color vibrancy) as the technical root cause of Samsung's competitive underperformance in this cycle.
insights.locator: Samsung’s anti-glare tech proved to be an Achilles’ heel

insights.claim: On benchmark testing, the R95H met all BT.2020 color gamut specs but showed less noticeable skin-tone variance than the LG Micro RGB Evo, and picture-mode adjustments moved the needle less than on the LG.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Quantifies the performance gap via standardized benchmarking (Spears & Munsil), giving a technical basis for the review's competitive ranking.
insights.locator: While the R95H met all of the BT.2020 color gamut specs

insights.claim: The R95H's dedicated AI Soccer Mode Pro made soccer game colors and clarity look 'phenomenal,' avoiding the grayed-out 'Netflix look' seen elsewhere on the TV.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Shows Samsung differentiating via sport-specific AI picture modes as a feature-level competitive lever even where base picture quality lags.
insights.quote: The colors and clarity looked phenomenal.
insights.locator: Ironically, World Cup soccer games didn’t have that grayed-out look

insights.claim: Samsung uses its proprietary Tizen OS rather than Google TV, and the reviewer had to work around a Netflix app installation error that Samsung was investigating.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Software platform friction (proprietary OS, app bugs) is a differentiator against competitors like Hisense and Sony that have moved to Google TV.
insights.locator: Samsung uses its own operating system, called Tizen OS
